data.title: Costco Q1 Earnings: Nice Double Beat But Not Enough Growth
data.description:
This past Thursday, Costco Wholesale (COST) released its fiscal first quarter earnings, beating analyst revenue and earnings estimates by a fairly wide margin, despite not growing at a particularly fast pace. The company’s release revealed continued growth in same store sales, and shared plans for future growth and expansion.

Costco has long been an investor fan favorite due to its hard-to-replicate economics and exceptional corporate ethics. The company has always scored well on labor relations, pricing, and corporate governance metrics, giving it a reputation as a company doing good in the world.

The above summary of Costco’s advantages explains why many investors like the stock. However, such advantages do not necessarily justify a 47 times earnings multiple, which COST stock trades at right now. It would take many years for Costco to return shareholders their investment if the company were to stop growing—and that’s not even taking into account opportunity costs. Now, I’m not saying that Costco actually is going to stop growing. The company plans to open 30 new warehouses per year going forward, 3.2% of the current total. That should supply some top-line growth, as should the continued growth in same-store sales. However, Costco hasn’t been growing at rates that normally command growth stock multiples—which 47 times earnings definitely is.

For the longest time, Costco investors were adamant that the company’s stock would keep going up indefinitely despite the peculiar mismatch between its growth rates and its earnings multiple. Lately, however, the stock has been disappointing shareholders. COST stock has declined 11% in price over the last 12 months, vastly underperforming the S&P 500. It would seem like the stock’s multiples are starting to matter.

When I last covered Costco stock, I rated it a hold on the grounds that, while the underlying company was a great business, the stock itself was too pricey. The rating ended up being a partial success. While COST stock has risen in price and delivered a 22% total return since my article was published, it has lagged behind the S&P 500, which has delivered a 32.5% total return in the same period. In this article I explain why I’m maintaining my COST rating at hold following the company’s fiscal first quarter earnings release.

Earnings Recap

Costco’s third quarter performance was a respectable showing, featuring beats on both revenue and earnings per share (EPS). Some standout metrics included:

  • $71.97 billion in revenue, up 8.2% (beat by $190 million).

  • $4.50 in diluted EPS, up 11.3% year over year (beat by $0.23).

  • 6.4% same-store sales growth, down from prior quarters.

  • $2.46 billion in operating income, up 12%.

  • $2 billion in net income, up 11.3%.

  • $2.69 billion in cash from operations (CFO), up 44%.

  • A 2.9% net margin.

  • 5.9% same store sales growth in the U.S.

  • 9% same-store sales growth in Canada.

As you can see, it was another quarter of substantial growth for Costco—though not rapid growth except for in CFO. The company’s profit margin of only 2.9% might appear pretty slim, but remember that it has long been a part of Costco’s strategy to operate at slim merchandise margins, allowing it to collect membership fees that are nearly pure profit.

Future Prospects

Particularly interesting in Costco’s first quarter release was the guidance. The company said that it planned to open 30 warehouses (retail locations) per year, an increase of 3.25% on this year’s 923. Reports indicate that Costco’s average revenue per store is $250 million, so we could be looking at an additional $7.5 billion per year in sales here. A 2.9% margin on that is $217.5 million. So, we could see a substantial addition to Costco’s already substantial annual earnings in the years ahead.

How much of an addition?

The numbers above predict that the company will be earning $217.5 million in incremental earnings from opening warehouses, ignoring financing costs and capital expenditures. Such costs will reduce the amounts somewhat. With interest rates trending down, financing costs should not be too great. CAPEX could be quite a bit, though, as Costco owns its warehouses outright rather than leasing them. For this reason I think Costco can earn a healthy $200 million in incremental earnings per year over the next five years.

Valuation

Having looked at Costco’s recent earnings and estimated its future prospects, we can now move on to valuing the stock.

We can start with multiples. At today’s price ($884.47), Costco stock is richly valued by pretty much all commonly looked at multiples. It currently trades at 47 times trailing earnings. More multiples can be seen in the image below.

As you can see, COST trades at high multiples across the board, earning it an ‘F’ on valuation in Seeking Alpha Quant.

Next, we can try modeling Costco’s future earnings based on my forecast a few paragraphs back ($200 million in incremental earnings per year). The forecast produces the following estimates, assuming that Costco’s current share count (444 million) does not change.

Net income

EPS

TTM period

$8.3 billion

$18.69

2026

$8.5 billion

$19.14

2027

$8.7 billion

$19.59

2028

$8.9 billion

$20.04

2029

$9.1 billion

$20.49

2030

$9.3 billion

$20.94

So, what we get here is 2.3% CAGR growth in EPS. Based on that assumption, along with a 2% terminal growth rate and a 5% discount rate, we end up with a price target of $644 for COST. That's a significant downside to today’s price; however, the growth forecast I'm using is very conservative, based only on what the company explicitly told us to expect (30 new stores per year). It didn’t factor in possible same-store sales growth, because that is impossible to predict. It is likely that there will be some such growth in the years ahead, pushing the theoretical estimate above the $644 I’ve estimated. Accordingly, I’m keeping my COST rating on hold.

Risks

While I think Costco stock is fairly safe to hold today, the stock certainly isn’t without risks. To the contrary, it faces several known risks, including:

  • Financing costs. To open 30 new warehouses per year, Costco will have to borrow money, as the company’s $17 billion in cash and equivalents wouldn’t cover the costs. Interest on the new debt could be a problem if interest rates rise.

  • Inflation. Costco is somewhat vulnerable to rising input costs stemming from inflation, as it operates on razor-thin margins already. If inflation continues to be elevated and COST’s suppliers start demanding higher prices, then COST’s margins could get squeezed further still.

  • High competition in e-commerce. Costco is increasingly an online business. In its online/e-commerce segment, the company lacks some of the advantages of its brick and mortar warehouses (e.g., getting all customers on a membership plan), which, when combined with the generally high competition in e-commerce, could be bad news for shareholders.

The Bottom Line

As I wrote in my previous article on Costco, this company is definitely a very good one, with steady growth, a wide moat, and reasonable profitability. But at 47 times earnings, the company’s stock looks like it is possibly a little overvalued. So, COST is just a hold for me.

data.title: FirstEnergy: Making Good Progress While Putting Bribery Scandal Behind It
data.description:
Five years ago, shareholders in utility company FirstEnergy (FE) were rocked by a rogue CEO swept up in a bribery scandal in the state of Ohio that resulted in an order to pay $250 million in fines & refunds. As a result, FE's 10-year total returns have significantly lagged the broad SPDR XLU Utility ETF (XLU) - see graphic below. However, YTD FirstEnergy's total returns have traded roughly in-line with the XLU ETF despite sporting a superior dividend yield of 4.0%. So, continuing with my recent Seeking Alpha coverage on "electrons" - whether they are consumed by AI data-centers or generated by electric utility companies (see here, here, and here) - today I'll take a fresh look at FirstEnergy to see if the worst is behind the company and if it too can benefit from the thematic AI data-center boom that is boosting the fortunes of many companies across the utility sector.

Investment Thesis

FirstEnergy is a utility holding company that has 3.6 GW of electric power generation capacity as well as significant electric transmission, and electric distribution assets. The company serves 6.4 million customers across 5 states: Ohio, Pennsylvania, New York, Maryland, and New Jersey. The slide below, taken from the 2025 EEI Financial Conference presentation in November, is a summary of FE's utility footprint:

The company operates in three primary segments:

  • Distribution (51% of 2024 revenue & 45% of operating income)
  • Integrated (36%, 34%)
  • Stand-alone Transmission (13%, 21%)

FirstEnergy targets a 6-8% EPS CAGR over the long term with a dividend payout ratio in a range of 60-70%. With that as background, let's take a closer look to see how FirstEnergy has been performing of late, if the worst is behind it, and what the company's future may hold.

Earnings

FirstEnergy released Q3 earnings on October 22 and they were generally quite strong:

  • Q3 non-GAAP EPS of $0.83/share was a $0.06 beat.
  • Management increased FY25 core earnings guidance to the upper-end of its previous range: ($2.50-$2.56).
  • Increased the 2025 investment program by 10% to $5.5 billion in order to capture growth opportunities.
  • Reaffirmed its 6-8% CAGR target for core earnings (2025 through 2029).

On the Q3 conference call, Brian Tierney - First Energy's CEO, President & Chair of the Board - cited a 30% YOY increase in capital deployed into regulated utilities through the first nine months of FY25 as the primary reason behind the increase in investment capital. He also said:

Load growth from data centers continues to transform our industry. FirstEnergy service territory, expertise and assets are ideally positioned to support the remarkable demand growth and economic opportunity within and adjacent to our footprint.

and

Our long-term pipeline of demand, which includes interconnection requests from serious and reputable customers has nearly doubled since our fourth quarter earnings call in February.

Despite ongoing regulatory issues in Ohio, FE's ROE profile has significantly improved:

As you can see from the right side of the graphic, Ohio is still a drag, but FE has three business lines that have an attractive 10%+ ROE.

Moving Forward

Data center demand of 3.8 GW was +30% since February, with pipeline demand of 11.7 GW up over 90%. As a result, it is clear that demand from both AI data centers and electric power transmission & distribution will be positive catalysts moving forward. FY2025 revenue should grow around 5% yoy, and perhaps as high as 5.5%, and roughly the same next year.

FE has reduced debt levels following an additional 30% sale in FirstEnergy Transmission ("FET") to Brookfield Super-Core Infrastructure Partners in 2023 for $3.5 billion. That transaction closed in Q1 of 2024 and enabled FE to reduce debt by $2.3 billion. Debt-to-capital at year-end 2024 was 63.9% compared to 69.7% at year-end 2023.

Going forward, less interest expense bodes well for shareholders in my opinion. Indeed, as previously mentioned, and after years of dividend growth stagnation, FE raised its dividend payout guidance to 60-70%. As a result, dividend growth off a 2024 base should grow at a CAGR of an estimated 5-6%.

Valuation

The chart below compares FE's valuation metrics with that of the broad utility sector as represented by the XLU ETF:

TTM P/E

Annual

Dividend

 Yield
FirstEnergy 19.2x $1.78 4.04%
XLU ETF 21.0x variable 2.55%

As you can see in the graphic , FirstEnergy trades at a valuation discount to the overall utility sector while paying a dividend that is nearly 1.5 percent higher than the XLU ETF. I believe both discounts are unwarranted.

Risks

The previous link to the Seeking Alpha "scandal" article reported that PUCO (Public Utilities Commission of Ohio) said on Wednesday that "the company must still face regulatory consequences for its actions". However, as the ROE graphic above shows, TTM ROE in Ohio is already a very low 4.7%, with a revenue adjustment expected. My point here is that it is not clear to me that ROE in Ohio can get much worse than it already is.

Clearly there are industry wide concerns about the AI data-center boom in terms of its dependability & sustainability. Any significant downturn in the AI infrastructure boom could negatively impact FE's growth rate going forward, as would a slowing economy.

In addition, higher interest rates could negatively impact FE's cost-of-capital and therefore the company's profitability.

Summary & Conclusion

I started out this article thinking I would likely put a SELL on FirstEnergy due to its scandalous history. However, that was 5 years ago, the company has a new CEO, and has made several asset sales to improve its balance sheet. Meantime, concerns about Ohio regulatory concerns may be overblown in my opinion. I say that because it is doubtful the PUCO is going to harshly penalize FE much further and to such an extent as to impede the company's ability to allocate growth capital in order to keep utility rates low for Ohio consumers and to enable Ohio to claim its share of the AI data-center boom. While I could certainly be wrong about that, the generous 4.0% yield is attractive, the utility sector in general appears to be a relatively safe place to invest given the macroeconomic environment, and I think the worst is behind First Energy.

For income-oriented utility investors, I rate FirstEnergy stock a BUY here.

data.title: The Ultimate Guide For The Netflix-Warner Bros. Discovery Merger: Strategic Rationale, Regulatory Hurdles, Bidding War
data.description:
What's an easy way of becoming HBO faster than HBO becoming Netflix? Buy HBO, of course!

Netflix Inc. (NFLX) announced it will buy Warner Bros. Studios & HBO in a deal worth in excess of $82 billion.

This article will discuss lessons learned from the Warner Bros. Discovery Inc. (WBD) investment case (which I covered and was bullish on since 2022), the strategic rationale of the merger and what it means for Netflix long-term, and how investors should approach the new deal.

I understand there is much debate over the bidding war involving Netflix and Paramount Skydance Corporation (PSKY). I will discuss why media coverage is similar to the noise that followed WBD for much of its life. Investors will be well-served to ignore it.

The Biggest Takeaway from WBD Investment Is That The Power of Brands Is Waning

I think the timeless lesson one can take away from investing in WBD is the value of patience and learning to drown out outside noise. For virtually its entire life, WBD and its management were under heavy criticism on Wall St. and in the media in general. It was for a host of reasons, such as the rationale of the merger, the heavy debt load, scrapping movies and cutting costs, chord-cutting, and losing out on the NBA, among others. The shares were down more than 70% from when the combined entity started trading. Even more brutal was that this drawdown lasted for more than 3 years. Ultimately, the focus on the value of IP rather than distribution challenges and trusting one's analysis rather than succumbing to the pressures of outside noise were key to maintaining a bullish view and ultimately making a decent return.

Having been bullish on WBD since I initiated coverage in 2022 (and since the merger was announced), I think it's important for me, however, to highlight that I was also wrong. I definitely overestimated its earning power as a standalone company and underestimated the speed with which cord-cutting would take place. Luckily, I did realize that the power of IP provided a margin of safety, and that's why this ended up being a satisfactory investment.

Ironically, I brushed on the Idea of Netflix buying WBD as far back as 2022. That was not a prediction by any means, but rather an illustration that media companies are primarily IP companies rather than distribution companies. In essence, WBD was a very safe investment once the stock plummeted. The bull case was that they would build a big streaming service and become the new Netflix. The bear case was that they would fail and sell the IP to a scaled distribution company that could monetize that IP better. Either way, the stock was worth multiples of what it was trading for most of its life post-merger. This proved the IP thesis correct. Because the company had durable intellectual property, it offered a sufficient margin of safety as it was always worth more to a scaled player.

However, it also proved that having a powerful brand alone was not enough to become a scaled player, as was evident by the Max product rollout, showing it was difficult to do this without help from wholesale distribution channels. As a result, it isn't entirely clear to me that the thesis was correct after all. Nonetheless, my 2022 article does a decent job of showcasing the synergies of the Netflix-WBD merger despite being written three years ago.

As for the difficulty of bringing to life a DTC business from the womb of a strong brand, I covered this dynamic in Shopify and the Streaming Analogy. The argument was basically that churn and customer acquisition cost made building a scaled DTC product difficult, especially if it already had scaled competitors. Brands were a feature, not a product.

And so the biggest takeaway for me from the WBD investment seems a little counterintuitive. The age of the internet, and soon AI, proves that content is not King and distribution rules. Now, this probably doesn't mean much for WBD, but it could have implications for other media players. In other words, the success of the WBD investment is likely not replicable with other seemingly similar investments, as the more viewing goes online and the more AI content creation proliferates, the weaker the remaining standalone brands will be.

Now that WB and HBO's invaluable IP is with a distribution platform that could leverage it, Netflix sits in a terrific position.

Netflix's Merger with WBD is Not Bad News for Its Stock

Netflix gets three main benefits out of the deal, which were framed brilliantly by Co-CEO Greg Peters in a recent Investor Conference. He referred to them as "centers of value."

The first part is there's a bunch of titles that the Warner teams have that we think that there's more value to unlock in. And we think we have exactly the platform to go do that. We've got incredible reach. We've got a project experience that maximizes the value and content. This is the business that we are in. Essentially, we are constantly in the business of evaluating various different licensing opportunities for titles and then trying to figure out how do we maximize the value of that asset on our platform.

We have seen Netflix run that playbook before. One of the biggest examples of that was the success of Manifest. The show was canceled by NBC before Netflix revived it with huge viewership and critical acclaim.

A similar dynamic happened with Breaking Bad. The show's creator credited Netflix with the show's success, saying the show came close to being canceled after season 2.

This was true for a show like Suits and many others, which reveals a pattern: some shows were more valuable on Netflix than they were on other platforms.

And now that Netflix gets a phenomenal library from WB and HBO, they will be able to present new shows to massive audiences at higher margins than by licensing them. And these shows will also be much more popular than they were on HBO Max. That means that Netflix will be able to increase engagement without increasing content spend, which can drive subscription revenue, ad revenue, and margins.

The other component is around HBO, the brand and the service. And HBO is an incredible brand with an incredible history. It speaks prestige TV. We want to double down on that concept. And then we think that, that gives us an additional lever, additional tool essentially to think about how do we propose plans and offerings for different consumers that give them the right kind of entertainment they want at the right price point.

This seems to suggest that Netflix will offer an add-on prestige TV tier using the brand of HBO. It is also likely the company will offer customers a choice as to how to subscribe: just HBO, just Netflix, or a bundle of the two.

This means that Netflix will finally realize the value of taking HBO global, direct to consumer, which should be fantastic for Netflix shareholders by itself.

If that leads to higher engagement, then the company could also start increasing prices strategically and would probably see margins expand. That will take longer to materialize in my view, as Netflix will inherit obligations with cable providers around the world that they are likely to honor.

On pricing, we no longer get Netflix's ARPU by region after the company stopped reporting subscribers. We have WBD's streaming ARPU of $10.4 in the US and $3.7 globally. Given Netflix's subscription prices, one could expect bundled prices in the US to be below $30 to start, depending on how much consumer surplus Netflix wants to take.

The good news is Netflix will have an easier time getting pricing right, as they have good data on how many "super fan" customers subscribe to both services, and that will be the ceiling for prices. They can then price below that, depending on how much consumer surplus they'd like to take.

There is also some optionality in the deal for Netflix shareholders in the form of the new hits the company can create with existing IP, as well as with the gaming studios.

There is, however, a benefit of the deal that its CEOs did Not Mention in the event, and it could be what propels Netflix to the $1 trillion valuation.

Netflix's New Plan: Become YouTube Before YouTube Becomes Netflix

Let's consider some of the Netflix headlines reported on Seeking Alpha in H2 of 2025:

Netflix in talks to license video podcasts from iHeartMedia.

Netflix to carry video podcasts from Spotify.

Netflix to launch a NASA feed.

Netflix strikes programmatic ads deal with Amazon.

Netflix is looking to win UEFA Champions League rights.

The one thing that jumps out to me is that these moves all seem to be designed to position Netflix as a video platform closer to YouTube rather than a premium-scripted video platform.

The way I see it, Netflix and YouTube are in a race. Both are trying to get to the other, and whoever gets there first will have the most lucrative business in the history of media.

For YouTube, they have the benefit of 2.5 billion accounts. The company streamed a billion hours a day in the US in 2024. The fact that they don't commission the production of their content also means they have a highly scalable model.

The downside is that their margins are relatively low. They have to pay 60% of ad revenue out of the gate to their content creators, which means a gross margin of at least 40%, compared to Netflix's 48%. That doesn't factor in costs like cloud hosting and payment services, which are in Netflix's calculation, so the margins are likely even lower. MoffetNathanson estimates YouTube's operating margins at 14%. One way YouTube is looking to expand that margin is by promoting its subscription services. Whether that's YouTube Premium, Music, or TV. Their push into sports is also one that could boost margins, as they won't be paying 60% of their revenue as cost of goods sold in these business models.

Meanwhile, Netflix has nowhere near YouTube's scale. They have about 300 million households worldwide. But more striking is that their subscribers worldwide streamed 95 billion hours in 2025 H1. For context, YouTube viewers watched some 180 billion hours during the same period in the US alone.

But no one has to feel bad for Netflix, as what they lack in engagement, they more than make up for in margin. While YouTube's user-generated content is thought of as an advantage in competing with Netflix, it's actually less profitable, as the latter does not spend 60% of its revenue on content. Based on the latest earnings call, YouTube is on a $40 billion revenue run rate from ads (it's much harder to quantify its subscription revenue, and it's minuscule anyways). That means the company makes about $5.5 billion in operating income if you believe MoffetNathanson's estimate. Meanwhile, Netflix generated almost $13 billion in TTM operating income, and management has said they expect margins to expand even further over time from the current 29%.

The next step for Netflix is to try and go after YouTube's scale. In addition to the reports on Seeking Alpha listed above relating to going into sports and podcasts, they are actively trying to lure top YouTube creators on to their platform in addition to producing movies starring social media stars. Most likely they won't maintain their margins, but if they can even match YouTube's engagement in the US, Netflix's operating profit can explode, especially since performance advertising is no longer a duopoly.

But how does acquiring WBD fit into that strategy? Combined, the companies will spend $30 billion on content. Note that WBD's content spend is profitable as well, so the revenue synergies are there. That content spend plus the library will allow Netflix to shift a lot of its spending towards live content and higher-quality YouTube-like content. This means the acquisition enables Netflix to maximize its scripted content appeal through tentpole shows and movies that attract subscribers, while delivering the low-cost content that drives engagement and keeps them. Sounds familiar? This was the rationale behind the WBD merger.

If Netflix beats YouTube to that, they could have as much as $200 billion in revenue. A $100 billion in subscription revenue and another $100 billion in ad revenue, with as much as $35 billion in net income. Even then, there would still be optionality in the consumer products and experiences verticals, which Netflix has yet to exploit.

Regulatory Risk of Netflix-WBD Merger Isn't High

The key detail I see missing in much of the discussion on this topic is that the burden of proof falls on regulators, not Netflix.

One of the key points, for example, is what the definition of the market Netflix competes in is. If the market is premium scripted content streaming, then Netflix will be deemed a monopoly; if it is TV in general or entertainment more broadly, then the deal has a higher chance of passing.

The thing is, that it is not how the question should be framed. The question is, can regulators prove that Netflix competes only in the premium scripted content streaming market?

If regulators cannot prove that, they will not be able to block the deal. Once you frame the question this way (which is how it will be looked at by regulators and in court), it becomes obvious that the deal will pass.

The main reason is that Netflix will be able to show through internal emails and other means that they do discuss competing with companies outside that market.

For example, consider this report, which notes that streamers sometimes criticize shows as not being second-screen enough. It clearly shows that Netflix does compete with platforms like Instagram and TikTok for users' attention. I do not see how regulators can prove that this is not the case, and as a result, I believe they will not be able to define the market as strictly premium scripted content streaming.

And if that is not convincing, check this quote from Mark Zuckerberg that absolutely shows executives don't think of competition in such narrow terms:

for Americans still almost half the time that they spend on screens is TVs more than more wphones or computers so... a lot of people are still just watching you know cable or different things like that and so when you think about new experiences I actually think the first thing that they're gonna go do is eat TV right and like and kind of the more passive things so you know when people talk about being worried about, you know, the time that people are spending in different kind of social experiences. I mean the time has to come from somewhere, I think it's worth looking at where it's coming from. If it's coming from sleep that's probably not great. If it's coming from exercise, like I wouldn't be that happy with that. If it's coming from TV I'm pretty fine with that right I think like I mean that's actually maybe a net improvement in well-being for people overall if you're shifting from this more beta kind of consuming state to just being actually actively engaged potentially building relationships and there's just a ton of TV time to eat

If the CEO of Meta himself is clearly saying he is competing with TV and cable for customers' time, how can regulators claim Netflix is not competing with Meta as well? Surely the market cannot be just premium scripted content streaming and more broadly includes social media apps and other forms of TV. Netflix doesn't even need such a broad definition; they'd be able to get the deal through if the market was all of TV. Greg Peters highlighted that in the most recent investor conference:

if you say, okay, well, you're going to go buy HBO and HBO Max, put that viewing on the list, this is what that would look like.

We go from 8% of view hours today in the United States to 9%. So we're still behind YouTube at 13%. And potentially worth noting that we would be behind what would be if Paramount combined with WBD, them at 14%. So we think that there's a really strong fundamentals-based case here for why regulators should approve this deal.

This is the slide he was referring to in the quote:

And even if Netflix's share in premium streaming is high, the availability of multiple competitors and substitutes trying to take TV time away puts a ceiling on its ability to raise prices.

A second advantage is that Netflix does not have a studio, and so, unlike Paramount, will not be taking a studio out of the market. This is referred to as a vertical merger, and is generally looked at favorably by regulators because of the elimination of double marginalization. There is also a horizontal merger in the form of the two streaming services, which regulators would look at. One way Netflix could get around it is to spin off HBO Max and keep the library and studios to preserve the status of the deal as a vertical merger, something I doubt regulators would be keen on, given HBO Max stands no chance without the studio and library. Regulators could, however, seek remedies that would see Netflix not shut down HBO Max and keep its unique IP exclusive to it. Even that seems like a stretch at this point, but even then, Netflix will happily agree, knowing it will bundle the two offerings anyway.

As for the fear that Washington favors one particular bidder over the other. I steer clear of politics, but given that it is relevant to the investment case, I'd simply point out that the US is renowned for its developed market and institutions. Such talk seems to be something a 24-hour news cycle likes to talk about but will ultimately have little significance.

Netflix's Deal for WBD is Superior to Paramount's

There is also debate on Wall St. whether Paramount's bid is superior. I believe that such an evaluation is dependent on how much upside investors want. If investors have no interest in longer-term participation, then getting $30 now will be viewed as superior. But if you believe the case I'm building for Netflix if it acquires WBD, then that 4.5% in Netflix shares will be worth more than $4.5, and that $30/share offer from Paramount won't come even close to matching the Netflix deal.

The Investor's Guideline for Paramount's Attempt to Scupper the Netflix-WBD Deal

It is worth highlighting that it is very rare for hostile takeovers to win. Media reporting will make it much closer than it is, given their interest in attracting eyeballs, and that could have a negative influence on investors. So here is a breakdown of why Netflix will win this deal and of Paramount's options.

Let's start with Paramount's tender offer. The most crucial point to know about it is that it is contingent on the failure of the WBD shareholder vote regarding the merger with Netflix. The tender itself is not an alternative to the deal; it only comes into effect if the vote fails.

As a result, investors should not be influenced by any media reports of some institutional investor deciding to tender their WBD shares. That will have no bearing on the outcome.

The question right now is, can Netflix and WBD management convince holders of more than 50% of the shares outstanding to vote in favor of the deal? If they get that over the line, it will not matter that a given investor decided to tender their shares.

Almost 25% of WBD's outstanding shares are held by Vanguard, BlackRock, and State Street. I read their voting guidelines, and basically, they all say that, as long as there is no evidence of conflict of interest or poor decision-making by management, they vote in favor of the management recommendation, as it's their fiduciary responsibility. Currently, the two offers on the table are too similar in terms of price to justify voting against management.

They also vote based on their assessment of what works for long-term shareholder value. As things stand, it seems very likely they will vote in favor of the deal, as WBD management will be able to provide them with all the evidence that the process was fair and in favor of long-term shareholder value. Most of the time, the Big 3 tend to vote with management. That is especially true if the decision was unanimous. Realistically, WBD management is really only trying to win 25% of the remaining votes.

Until then, expect a lot of rumors and reporting of an unfair and biased process as Paramount looks to sway at least one of the Big 3.

If that does not work, Paramount will look to increase its bid. I expect Netflix to continue fighting to get WBD even then. How they fight will depend on their expectations of the shareholder vote. If they feel confident they can win the vote, they might decide against upping their bid. But if they need to, I fully expect Netflix to up its bid in return. Note that Netflix will still hold the advantage in this case, as Paramount will always need to pay a breakup fee that Netflix doesn't have to. That breakup fee is about $1.2-$1.3 a share.

The only way I see Paramount winning this deal is if they can win back management or make an offer that would see some board members defect. I consider that a remote possibility, as I expect Netflix to match even a $35/share offer from Paramount. It is only if Paramount offers something in the region of $40/share that I see Netflix walking away. Obviously the WBD board raised concerns over the financing of the deal, so some shareholders could still choose to vote for a Netflix deal.

If, however, Paramount does not engage in a bidding war that wins WBD management over and continues to go the hostile route, Zaslav and Co. will have more than enough cards to play to beat Paramount, including adopting a poison pill.

My recommendation is that investors buy WBD and Netflix. I believe WBD shares right now are a win-win, as they give you exposure to Netflix shares if the deal goes through, in addition to exposure to further upside gains if we do go to a bidding war. The shares will also provide some stability in a broader market downturn. I find that combination to be very appealing.

As for Netflix, I do rate it a buy in anticipation of the merger closing. I view the pressure the stock is under currently as an opportunity. I expect shares to remain muted as this merger plays out and would use that as an opportunity to add more shares. My thesis could change if Netflix does not end up with WBD.

I do not see a reason to own Paramount shares at the moment, and even if they do buy WBD, I do not see their competitive position improving much.

data.title: China Reaffirms Tight Grip On Gold Market, Ushering In A New Monetary Era
data.description:
Without a doubt, the Chinese central bank (PBoC) is still the leading single entity that is driving up the gold price to record highs, year-to-date by more than 55 percent.

In the third quarter of 2025, the PBoC’s gold purchases (reported and unreported) accounted for 118 tonnes, up 39% MoM and 55% YoY, according to my long-time methodology1 (now copied by Goldman Sachs, Bloomberg, MarketWatch, The Washington Post, TIME magazine, Financial Times, Financiele Dagblad, and El País, to name a few).

Chart 1. Reported central bank gold buying versus estimates by the World Gold Council on central bank gold buying based on field research (reported and unreported buying). The majority of unreported buying must be ascribed to China.

My estimated total for Chinese monetary gold reserves stands at 5,411 tonnes in Q3, versus 2,304 tonnes reported by the central bank of China to the IMF.

Why is China buying so much gold?

It’s because China is the second-largest economy globally, and due to the weaponization of the dollar since the Ukraine war in 2022, the vast, covert buying spree by China and countries like Saudi Arabia should not be viewed as a hedge against the dollar but as a replacement for the dollar.

The mBridge Gold Standard

For the better part of the past 80 years, the U.S. dollar has functioned as the world’s trade and reserve currency. This setup gave the United States the exorbitant privilege of being able to print money to pay for imports, even though America’s manufacturing base has been eroded as a consequence.

China aims to establish an alternative to the U.S. dollar while seeking to avoid the risks associated with issuing its own reserve currency.

On November 19, 2025, the chairman of the Central Bank of the United Arab Emirates completed a landmark digital currency transaction during a meeting with the governor of the People’s Bank of China, formally inaugurating project mBridge.

The platform allows participating countries with established digital currencies to conduct bilateral trade in their own currencies, bypassing the U.S. dollar.

For the “mBridge gold standard” to be fulfilled, any surplus of local currency accumulated through trade must be directly exchangeable in a liquid gold market. Furthermore, it requires a new international gold vaulting and clearinghouse network.

Chart 2. In a historical shift, Eastern countries that before 2022 were price sensitive are now driving gold higher.

China will be the largest, or one of the largest, trading partners of countries participating in mBridge, and so the renminbi will be a dominant trade currency in the arrangement. This is why the Chinese are developing the Shanghai International Gold Exchange (SGEI).

The SGEI was launched in the Shanghai Free Trade Zone (FTZ) in 2014 and recently opened its first offshore vault in Hong Kong. The idea is to open additional vaults abroad.

The international vaulting system will likely be developed in accordance with the extensive network of repositories that constitute the SGEI system within the Chinese domestic market.

In the last SGEI Annual Report, it states that the SGEI adopts a “centralization, netting, and multi-tiered” clearing model with 18 certified margin custodian banks offering 70 certified vaults in 36 cities throughout the mainland.

Gold Is Shifting the Distribution of Global Reserves

As central banks aggressively load up on gold and move the price to new all-time highs, global gold reserves are rising to the detriment of the dollar. By Q3, my estimated total of central banks’ monetary gold reserves accounted for 40,225 tonnes, 49 percent of which is owned by countries outside the West. In the 1990s, this percentage was 25 percent.

Chart 3. The West comprises the US, UK, Western Europe, and Australia.

Due to the actions of the PBoC in increasing prices, monetary gold now accounts for almost 28 percent of global international reserves. This is a truly monumental increase from 21 percent in 2024.

Chart 4. Because gold has appreciated against all reserve currencies since Q3, gold’s “live” share of reserves is actually closer to 30 percent.

Notes

  • My most important analyses on the covert PBoC’s gold buying are:
  • Estimating the True Size of China’s Gold Reserves (February 2023)
  • PBoC Gold Conduit Revealed—Chinese Central Bank Did Not Stop Buying Gold in May (July 2024)
  • Chinese Central Bank Just Secretly Bought 60 Tonnes of Gold (November 2024)
  • China’s Gold Reserves Going Through the Roof (April 2025)

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
